quotes.text: You're not scared of me. That's going to be a problem for both of us.
quotes.speaker: The Silence of the Lambs (1991)
quotes.context: Hannibal Lecter, studying Clarice during one of their early sessions
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the calm inversion of threat, casting the victim's courage as their liability, is very Lecter in rhythm and attitude. The giveaway is the phrase 'for both of us,' which implies a partnership Lecter would be too controlled to offer this early in their relationship.

quotes.text: I promised my father I'd stay out of trouble. I never said anything about staying out of fights.
quotes.speaker: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid (1969)
quotes.context: Butch Cassidy, grinning before a brawl
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — William Goldman's script loves exactly this kind of quick, self-delighting wordplay from Butch, and the casual daddy-reference fits the tone. The subtle giveaway is the two-part structure; it's a touch more constructed than Goldman's best throwaway lines, which feel more accidental.

quotes.text: Get your stuff. We're not sleeping in a car that smells like this.
quotes.speaker: Planes, Trains and Automobiles (1987)
quotes.context: Neal Page, exasperated after another of Del's mishaps
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Neal's clipped, barely-contained irritation at Del's chaos is exactly the register Steve Martin plays throughout the film. The subtle giveaway is 'stuff'; Neal speaks just a touch more precisely than that, even when he's furious.

quotes.text: Back off, man. I'm a scientist.
quotes.speaker: Ghostbusters (1984)
quotes.context: Dr. Peter Venkman (Bill Murray) defends his research methods to a skeptical librarian.

quotes.text: The name on the building doesn't mean anybody inside it is honest.
quotes.speaker: The Maltese Falcon (1941)
quotes.context: Sam Spade, warning a client early in the picture
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Spade's blunt street-philosophy and the clipped Hammett syntax feel authentic to Huston's script. The subtle giveaway is that it reads a hair too aphoristic and self-contained; real Spade lines are usually aimed at a specific person's specific lie rather than delivered as general wisdom.

quotes.text: Funny business, a woman's career. The things you drop on your way up the ladder so you can move faster. You forget you'll need them again when you get back to being a woman.
quotes.speaker: All About Eve (1950)
quotes.context: Aging Broadway star Margo Channing (Bette Davis) reflecting on the costs of ambition.
